title: Excluded volume and molecular field in the Lennard-Jones fluid: a modified first-order perturbation theory
authors: A. Trokhymchuk, V. Hordiichuk, R. Melnyk, I. Nezbeda
abstract: The equation of state and, more generally, the thermodynamics of the Lennard-Jones fluid have long served as a benchmark problem in the statistical theory of fluids. Among available theoretical approaches, first-order perturbation theory occupies a special position: only at this level does the correction to the Helmholtz free energy admit an exact statistical-mechanical expression. In this work, we present a systematic, simulation-based assessment of a non-classical first-order perturbation theory in which the reference system incorporates the entire short-range part of the interaction, while the perturbation is confined to the remaining long-range tail. We show that this range-based decomposition transforms the perturbation contribution into a small, smoothly varying, near-mean-field quantity over a broad supercritical thermodynamic domain. When its density and temperature derivatives are consistently retained, the resulting equation of state reproduces high-accuracy reference data with excellent fidelity. The results demonstrate that the success of first-order perturbation theory is governed primarily by the physical content of the reference system and by the consistent treatment of its state dependence, rather than by the formal truncation order of the expansion.
summary: This paper proposes a new way to split the Lennard-Jones potential for use in first-order perturbation theory. Instead of splitting by interaction strength (repulsion vs. attraction), it splits by interaction range, putting all short-range interactions into the reference system. This makes the remaining perturbation term much smaller and easier to model as a mean-field-like quantity. The study proves that including the density derivatives of this new term is essential for an accurate and thermodynamically consistent description of the fluid.
main_problem: The failure of classical first-order perturbation theories (like WCA) to accurately describe the thermodynamics of Lennard-Jones fluids due to the overlap of the perturbation term with the region of strong structural correlations.
main_result: A non-classical approach using a range-based decomposition—where the reference system includes the entire short-range part of the potential—provides a highly accurate equation of state by transforming the perturbation into a small, near-mean-field quantity.
method: The authors use Molecular Dynamics simulations to evaluate a first-order perturbation theory where the reference system is defined by a short-range potential, and the long-range tail is treated as a perturbation, including necessary density and temperature derivatives for thermodynamic consistency.
model_or_system: The system is a Lennard-Jones (LJ) fluid, characterized by a pairwise additive potential with repulsive and attractive components, studied in the supercritical thermodynamic domain.
key_observables: Helmholtz free energy, pressure, internal energy, and the perturbation contribution a(T, rho).
important_parameters: Supercritical temperature and density ranges, Lennard-Jones parameters epsilon and sigma, and the decay parameters of the two-Yukawa reference potential.
main_assumptions: The perturbation contribution remains small and smoothly varying because it is restricted to the long-range tail, and the success of the theory depends on the reference system capturing the local structure.
figures_summary: Figure 1 compares CA and NCA decomposition strategies; Figure 2 shows a phase diagram of the reference system; Figure 3 and 4 show the density and temperature dependence of the perturbation term; Figure 5 and 6 demonstrate the accuracy of the resulting pressure and the necessity of the density-derivative term; Figure 7 shows the accuracy of the internal energy.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of classical perturbation theory limitations, proposes a non-classical range-based decomposition, details the simulation-based evaluation of the new reference system, presents the results for the perturbation term's behavior, and concludes by demonstrating thermodynamic consistency and accuracy against benchmark data.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Lennard-Jones potential u(r) (the thin solid line) and two choices of its decomposition. The left panel: The CA decomposition suggested within the WCA method – into the repulsive interaction (the thick solid line) and the attractive interaction (the thin dashed line). The right panel: The NCA decomposition suggested in Ref. [15] and used in present study – into the short-range interaction uSR(r) (the thick solid line) and the remaining attraction ∆u(r) (the thin dashed line). .

Figure 2. Vapor-liquid phase diagram of the system with short-range attraction uSR(r) according to Eq. (7) (the empty circles) from Ref. [15] in comparison against the same for the Lennard-Jones fluid (the solid triangles) from Ref. [16]. The empty small circles in supercritical region indicate thermodynamic states for which MD simulations were performed.

Figure 3. Dependence of the perturbation term a(T, ρ) on density for the set of fixed temperatures T ⋆shown in figure. The results of calculations according to Eq. (11) are marked by symbols. The thin solid curves correspond to the results of polynomial parametrization, Eq. (14), while the thick solid horizontal line presents the result of mean-field approximation by assuming gSR(r) = 1 in Eq. (11).

Figure 4. Dependence of the perturbation term a(T, ρ) on temperature for the set of fixed densities ρ⋆shown in figure. The results of calculations according to Eq. (11) are marked by symbols. The thin solid curves correspond to the results of polynomial parametrization, Eq. (14), while the thick solid horizontal line presents the result of mean-field approximation by assuming gSR(r) = 1 in Eq. (11).

Figure 5. Dependence of the pressure βp = p/kT on density for the set of fixed temperatures T ⋆= kT/ϵLJ. The solid lines are the results of of computer-based equation of state for LJ fluid [1], the filled symbols show results of Eq. (17).
Figure 6. The same as in Fig. 5 but with the open symbols corresponding to negligence of the density derivative in Eq. (17).

Figure 7. Dependence of the internal energy βE/N = E/NkT on temperature for the set of fixed densities ρ⋆= ρσ3 LJ shown in figure. The solid lines are the results of computer-based approach due to Kolafa and Nezbeda [1], the filled symbols show results of Eq. (18).
